title: Understanding the Driving Force of Evolution: Mutations and Genetic Variation

lesson.learn:
Mutations create variation by changing the DNA sequence that cells pass on. Most changes are small, and many have no obvious effect, but some alter proteins, gene regulation, or the timing of development. Over long periods, those differences become raw material for evolution.

That is why mutation is not just damage. It is also one of the main sources of biological diversity.
lesson.simpler: Mutations are small DNA changes. Some do nothing, some cause problems, and some create useful variation that evolution can act on.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Mutation creates variation by introducing new genetic differences into populations.

learnImageCredit.creator: Magnus Manske; upright version by Dietzel65; vector derivative by Tryphon

lesson.deeperDive:
Mutations can happen during DNA copying, from environmental damage, or through errors in repair. They may affect a single base, delete or add pieces of DNA, or rearrange larger chromosome segments. Germline mutations can be inherited, while somatic mutations stay in one body and can contribute to cancer or tissue mosaicism.

Natural selection does not create the variation first; mutation does. Selection then filters which variants spread in a population. That combination is one of the main engines of biological change.
lesson.funFact: Not every mutation is harmful; many simply become part of life’s genetic variety.
references: NCBI Bookshelf - DNA repair and mutation
